facade_colour: red-orange brick with buff/yellow brick polychromatic banding and voussoirs

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The properties retain original building features including a cross gabled roofline, mirrored facades, double-height bay windows and fenestration pattern. 153 Denison Avenue has also retained its brick cladding and wooden decorative wooden gable brackets. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: Local | Residential | Transition to Bellevue Park

photo_observations.decorative_details: Polychromatic brickwork with buff brick voussoirs over windows, decorative brick patterning in gable, brick corbelling
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Upper-level balcony/deck addition at rear, wooden railings added
photo_observations.notes: Excellent example of bay-and-gable semi-detached with retained decorative brick cladding and wooden gable brackets as noted in HCD statement

facade_description: 153 Denison Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double hung, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, bay window, original windows, string courses, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The properties retain original building features including a cross gabled roofline, mirrored facades, double-height bay windows and fenestration pattern. 153 Denison Avenue has also retained its brick cladding and wooden decorative wooden gable brackets. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
heritage_summary: 153 Denison Avenue has also retained its brick cladding and wooden decorative wooden gable brackets. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
